8.1.label: Question 8.1
8.1.synopsis: Development costs unrecouped, sales needed to meet total profit objectives, overheads not covered, less tangible effects such as worse future results from lack of market presence
8.1.question: Explain why poor sales of a particular product line would be a problem for the company, even if sales of other products were not affected.

Why poor sales of a product line is a pr: There will have been development costs, which will not be recouped.
It is likely that the company was requiring a certain level of sales of the new product to meet total profit objectives for the whole business.
So simply having the same sales volumes of the other products may be inadequate to cover the company's current level of overheads, at least to the level needed to produce the required profit as well.
There may be other, less tangible, effects. For example, the failure of the insurer to establish a presence in this particular market may lead to worse results in future years.

8.2.label: Question 8.2
8.2.synopsis: Policy handling systems are complex, tampering risks administrative errors, risk of corrupting the system leads to over-paying claims, omitting lapsed premiums, incorrect valuation reserves; separate system avoids this risk provided claim volumes not too large, but requires manual validation back to policy records
8.2.question: Explain why a stand-alone claims management system is likely to be preferable (from a practical point of view) to building the claims handling process into an existing main policy-handling system.

Advantages of a stand-alone claims manag: Policy handling systems are complex, and tampering with them can easily lead to administrative errors and other problems.
The risk of corrupting the system for the insurer could have enormous consequences, for example:
• omitting to deal with lapsed premium payments
• valuation records could be wrong so that the company could end up with an incorrect valuation reserve.
Keeping the claims system separate avoids this risk, and will work well provided the numbers of claims involved are not too large.
However, this will require the manual validation of claims back to policy records.

8.3.label: Question 8.3
8.3.synopsis: Individual competitors: public supervisory reports are audited and credible but too high-level and out of date. Market as a whole: survey results credible and available but figures affected by company differences and difficult to use, scarce if product is new, out of date and not independently checked. Population statistics: readily available and cheap but usually not relevant for insurance use, reliability poor, not in appropriate format, can be out of date. Insurance or population data from other countries: same as domestic data but less relevant and harder to check. Figures through reinsurers or consultants: accurate and detailed but expensive
8.3.question:
Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of the following sources of data for pricing a new product:
• individual competitors
• the market as a whole
• population statistics
• insurance statistics from other countries
• population statistics from other countries
• other data available through reinsurers or consultants.

Individual competitors: • figures in the public supervisory reports will be easily available
• figures in the supervisory reports are audited and hence should be accurate!
• data could be used to give information on trends
• figures will usually be at too high a level of detail to do any accurate pricing
• figures for a class of business will be shown in total, and so it may not be possible to get information at a product-level detail (unless the insurer writes only one product type within the class)
• reported figures may be out of date by the time they become available
Market as a whole: • the results of surveys are generally available to all companies who contribute to it
• data could be used to give information on trends
• figures will usually be at too high a level of detail to do any accurate pricing
• figures will be affected by differences in many factors between companies such as exposure, distribution methods, product differences, etc hence will be extremely difficult to use
• anything more detailed will be virtually impossible to obtain, for competitive reasons
• if the product is relatively new to the country, data will be scanty
• most reported figures may be out of date by the time they become available
• figures are unlikely to be checked, certainly not independently
Population statistics: • usually experience is not relevant for insurance use (because of very different exposure) and the conditions for 'claiming' may be very different from population incidence
• the reliability of the figures may be poor, due to problems such as inconsistencies in sickness definitions, double counting of cases, etc
• data often not available in the appropriate format (eg electronically)
• data can be out of date before they are published
Insurance or population data from other: Same comments as above for domestic data, plus:
• overseas data are likely to be less relevant than those from domestic sources
• accuracy and reliability of data may be questionable and less easy to check.
Figures through reinsurers or consultant: • reinsurers and consultants will be keen to ensure accuracy of data – otherwise their reputation will suffer and, depending on the form of reinsurance, reinsurers will also lose profits on the business if premiums are inaccurate
• data are often more detailed and relevant than those that are publicly available, or the reinsurer / consultant is usually able to help with making the required adjustments
• the expense of obtaining the data, ie cost of reinsurance or consultant fees

8.4.label: Question 8.4
8.4.synopsis: More difficult to validate claims (need more evidence than a death certificate), greater understanding of medical conditions needed, condition for claiming often not clear cut requiring more judgement, more varied and complex reasons for claiming, continual assessment for IP/LTCI with regular claimant contact and recovery management, long-duration PMI monitoring, multiple claims history for IP/LTCI/PMI, dealing sensitively and fairly with ill but potentially invalid claimants, providing medical treatment advice, higher data detail required for pricing and forecasting, data protection and medical information sharing compliance
8.4.question: A claims assessor, previously working on life assurance products, is now working on a range of health products. Outline the additional requirements the assessor will face.

Additional requirements for a health cla: It will be more difficult to validate claims. They will need to collect and use more evidence than just a death certificate, especially if medical underwriting is carried out at the claims stage (eg moratorium underwriting is being used).
They will need a greater understanding of medical conditions to be able to assess a claim.
The condition for claiming will often not be clear cut, and so more judgement will be required.
The reasons for claiming will be more varied and complex than for life insurance.

For IP insurance and LTCI, the insurer will need to continually assess whether a claim can continue, and so the assessor may be in touch with the claimant regularly during a claim, and then deal with the recovery.
Similarly for PMI, treatment may last a long time, and the insurer may wish to monitor this.
For IP insurance, LTCI and PMI customers may claim several times, and so previous claims history should be borne in mind when dealing with the customer.
The assessor will need to be able to deal sensitively and fairly with claimants. They are likely to be ill but some claims may not be valid.
In addition, some claimants will be looking to the insurer for advice, eg where to have medical treatment.
A higher level of detail in the data may be required than for life insurance for pricing, forecasting, etc. It will therefore be important that the assessor records claims details accurately.

Sensitive information will often be required at claims stage. It will therefore be important that the assessor complies with the requirements of any data protection legislation or restrictions around the sharing of medical information.
